does: This paper introduces a reinforcement learning-based method for generating adversarial jailbreak prompts against aligned large language models. The approach uses a small surrogate model and only requires inference API access to the target model, optimizing adversarial triggers through a reinforcement learning framework with a BERTScore-based reward function. Rather than relying on manual prompt engineering or gradient-based methods that demand full model access, the RL approach automatically discovers diverse, transferable adversarial triggers capable of bypassing safety alignments. The method is evaluated on black-box LLM APIs, demonstrating improved attack success rates and transferability compared to existing jailbreaking techniques while maintaining practical constraints on model access and computational requirements.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of adversarial attacks research targeting aligned language models, responding to limitations in prior jailbreaking approaches like soft embedding prompts, manually crafted prompts, and gradient-based automatic prompt generation. It extends adversarial attack methodology from computer vision and NLP into the alignment safety domain, leveraging reinforcement learning frameworks previously applied to policy optimization. The paper connects to broader literature on alignment robustness, adversarial robustness of deep learning systems, and black-box attack strategies that operate under realistic constraints. It also draws on transfer learning concepts to address the challenge of creating attacks that generalize across different target models, and employs BERTScore, a neural semantic similarity metric, as a principled reward signal rather than relying solely on manual prompt curation.
enables: This work enables future research into understanding fundamental vulnerabilities in alignment techniques and the robustness of RLHF and similar safety training methods. By automating jailbreak discovery through RL, it provides tools for red-teaming and security evaluation of LLM deployments at scale. The approach could inform better defense mechanisms and more resilient alignment procedures by revealing systematic failure modes. It points toward developing adversarial robustness benchmarks for aligned models and exploring why certain semantic triggers persistently bypass alignment. Future work could investigate defenses against RL-based attacks, study the trade-offs between alignment robustness and utility, and extend the methodology to multimodal models, enabling comprehensive safety evaluation frameworks for increasingly capable AI systems.
